speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number FOUR, an interview with Edith Cimmer.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the second day, the German came. So I picked myself up. I didn't say anything. I went to the Gentile cemetery, and I laid down between those graves. I thought to myself that maybe the Germans, in my mind, they are not going to go to the cemetery to look for Jewish people. Maybe they're not that clever. And I laid there between the graves for a whole day until the evening.

speaker: interviewer
text: iN the evening, I took a whisk. I picked myself up, and I ran back to to the house, and the Germans already went away. So they asked me, where was I? I didn't say anything because I thought in case it might happen again, he doesn't have to know my secrets. And I made it. I survived. They didn't catch me. What time of year was this now?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was already there in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you stayed on these farms, what kind of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I milked the cows three times a day. I gave the pigs some food. I did housework. And then I worked in the field. That was in the fall. I digged potatoes. I did the corn with a-- I don't know how you call this-- with a sierp. It's like you take as many like that with a sierp. I did this. And then I learned how to knit. In three, four days, I made a sweater. I was number one in knitting. I knit for them sweaters. I knit mittens, gloves, skirts, hats for the men, scarves. I knit everything. So at night, I used to knit a lot of dresses. So I used to knit with-- I used to crochet and knit. And I was in one day, I made the first, first like this, first part. See, this is like right and this is left when you knit. When you knit it. I was very good in that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they treat you, that family?

speaker: interviewee
text: They treated me very nicely. Not to say that-- especially when they knew about the time a Jewish girl. And as I say, they were afraid for the Ukrainian. They were against, so they became very good to the Jewish people. And then they said, one day, it came a Jewish fellow. And he says, [NON-ENGLISH] to this man. You see, she is like-- he is a Jewish man, and I'm a Jewish girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got scared, then I ran away out on the barn, and I hided on top of-- in the hay. So he came, and he started to talk to me, the Jewish. He was already elderly man, maybe in the 60s. And I was already about 18, 17 years old at the time. So anyway, he came up, and we were talking, and he was hiding in the bunkers under the ground in [NON-ENGLISH] in the forest. So he start to talk to me. It's a small world. He is with the people that are related to me, my mother's cousins. He's the one wanted to marry my middle sister before the war. But she was-- she was 19. She was young. And she wanted my older sister should get married first. And she didn't want it. So he-- he survived. He is the uncle, my mother's-- his wife was my mother's sister-- the uncle's. So he survived, and one daughter survived with two little boys. A son survived with his wife. He had five children, and his wife-- this man, his wife and a child got killed. Another daughter got killed with a child. And they were killed, but the two sons and a daughter with the children, and their uncle, the father, their [INAUDIBLE]. So he says, I'm with Yehuda-- [NON-ENGLISH] was his name, Yehuda [NON-ENGLISH] was-- I'm staying with them. So I was so shocked. And I say, he's my relative. So he went over there underground, and he told them that he met relatives of mine. So this man, his son, he thought this is my sister because before the war, he wanted to marry my sister. And so he thought that she survived. So when he came-- so I said, no, that's me. My sister got killed. And his wife got killed. And he had a child nine months old. So he says, you know what? Maybe God-- OK, you're a lot younger than him, 17 years. So he said, you know what? He wants to take me out from the-- from the house. So they say the Polish people-- the Polish people, they say, the owner, [NON-ENGLISH]. She's not anymore a Jewish girl. [NON-ENGLISH]. That means that she's not anymore a Jewish girl. She is Polish. And we marry her. She's going to marry a Polish fellow, and she's going to become from ours. They were Catholic. And they kicked him out from the house. They didn't let him to take me to stay with them. They kicked him out from the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I couldn't do anything. So I ran outside, and he said-- we were talking-- and he said, on the second day, he came over, and that night, while they were asleep, I opened the window, and I ran away from the house through the window, and I went with them to their forest, and I slept already with them. Then it became misery already. Again, because here I was in the house. It was warm. I was fed. I was not hungry. It was a warm house. In there, it's already in the forest. It's cold. It's miserable. And again, I start with the lies again, pulling all over because we slept on the straw, me and my cousin. And then we start to go from house to house to beg some food. So the two-- one little boy was like-- he was born during the war. He was like nine months old. And they are apart. They are two sons. In fact, they are alive. One lives in West Bloomfield, and the other one lives in Livermore, California. And so when the German used to come-- so my uncle-- that's the father from my cousins-- used to take-- he had with him a bottle of whiskey. He used to take a tablespoon and give him a tablespoon of whiskey, the little baby. And he fell asleep because if he would cry, they would kill us all. In another bunker next to us, the lady had a little boy too. And while they were in the forest, they were digging for the Jew. And she put a piece of rag, like a piece of rag in the baby's mouth-- shouldn't cry. And she choked the baby. Got choked. Couldn't breathe because they were a long time in the forest. They were looking on top of their bunkers. And she couldn't take it out, and the baby couldn't breathe, and the baby choked. So while the baby choked, they saved about three, four families. But she buried the baby there, the mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was looking for the bunkers? The Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans with mostly the Gentile. The boys went with them, the people went with them to look for them because they left their homes. They had they left their goods and everything. So if we are still alive, then we're going to take everything away. Otherwise, everything is going to be belonging to them. They took over the whole households. So they wanted to-- and they became very nasty, very mean, the-- the people, the Polish people, the Ukrainian people because they wanted the land. They wanted their homes. They wanted whatever in the house, the furniture and everything. It became to them, so they didn't want them to be alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was there in the bunkers with my cousins. We went, me and the little boy-- he was 10 years old, the oldest one-- we went from house to house. Again, misery, slavery. We went to dig, you know, for this, and the Germans' airplanes saw us walking. So the airplanes went down all the way low, and they start to-- and I say his name is Fred Kendall-- and I say Fred, lie down, lie down. Don't this-- and they noticed us from the airplane, and they start to shoot on us from the airplane. So we lay under the forest, under a tree. And I said-- and he start to cry, oh, I'm not going to see my mother anymore. I won't see my mother anymore. They going to kill me. They're killing us. And I said, [NON-ENGLISH]-- that means Fred-- Fred, please don't pick up the head. You just dig your head into the ground. Then don't pick up because they're very low over us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he did, and they-- they were so low that the branches from the trees fell on us. They were so low. And I said, don't move. So he listened to me. He didn't move. And they were flying all around quite a few times, this area, and they didn't see anything, and they flew away. And we were alive. And we went already like, begging for something to the houses, begging for bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were there for a while. And then after they're there, these soldiers, the Americans came out and the England soldiers, and they're friends. They came out to help the Russians, because the Germans were already on the Leningrad. And they pulled already the Germans back to-- you know, they killed a lot of them on the Leningrad. There it was very cold and everything. The Germans-- I mean, the Russians and American soldiers, so they came and they freed us from the American soldiers. So how long were you in the bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in the bunkers, it was already after close to being liberated. When the American soldiers came out, and we heard already that we are already liberated. About a month, let's say. Because the Germans, they did not expect it. You know, they still had people in the concentrations that were still alive. And they needed for work. They did a lot of work. Like they-- they did a lot of-- Then it was organized Russian underground they called Kolpakov organization. Like you gave me to read in the book now. That's the biggest Russian partisans underground. And they did a lot of damages for the Germans. And a lot of Jewish boys that they survived, they went into this organization, to the Kolpakov. But under the [NON-ENGLISH]-- that means [INAUDIBLE] in Poland-- they surrounded them, the Germans, and they killed all the whole Russian group, the Kolpakov. They called Kolpakov, the organization underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in the bunker, did you ever have contact with any of the partisan groups?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did see him them, because they came to the villages, the partisan groups. And they came, the Russians, to the houses to take food and everything. And to be honest with you, I even they recognized a cousin of mine that he was in this group, the one that I showed you. He went to Israel after liberation. [INAUDIBLE] his name. That was my mother and his father were brother and sisters. And I hide it. I didn't want-- I didn't want to go to the organization. I'll tell you why. First of all, I heard that if you go over there, even my cousin-- I don't know, he had his wife and two children that Hitler killed-- you have to give in to them, especially to the main boss from the-- the organization, the underground, the partisan group. You have to give in. I had to have some sex with them. And I was afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thought to myself, no, I was hiding. They came to this house to rob, to have some food, to take everything they could-- milk and butter and bread, whatever they could take it. And I hid myself because he would recognize me. He lived in a different city from me, but he would recognize me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't want it. I didn't want to because I knew I heard that a lot of Jewish girls went with them, and they had to lay with them. Maybe-- maybe the life would have been easier, maybe with the food and everything. But I was afraid just because of that, that they would molest me and everything. I wouldn't have no other choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the conditions in the bunker? How many people and--?

speaker: interviewee
text: The conditions in the bunkers are like-- one bunker, we were like my cousin with the two children and I, and his brother that his wife got killed, and their father. He died, he was 104 years old. He died here in the United States in Livermore, California. So we were like, three, four, four, six in the bunker. Some bunkers, there were like two families. As I say, the baby got killed, so child couldn't breathe, so they got saved themselves. And every time the baby, you know, my cousin's little boy, he used to cry, so we used to give him a tablespoon with whiskey, and he fell asleep. And it was-- he is the one that [INAUDIBLE] saved the life, the uncle used to take care of him. And we went to the houses, or at night we went on the field, and we take the-- the corn that was already ripe. And we used to come home and peel it. We tore bunches, and we used to come home in the bunkers and peel it, and eat this dry corn. Or we ate mushrooms, the dry mushrooms, or like potatoes. So we dig outside. We used to steal from the farmers at night and eat their potatoes, dig potatoes. It was-- we were full of lice. We were full of lice. And we used to get up in the morning and take off the lice, and then to lie down again because we didn't bathe ourselves. We didn't. We couldn't, not anything. Until when the [NON-ENGLISH] it was already on the end when they-- already the American soldiers came in. And they already pulled the German back to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were-- like, all of us, we went to-- now, a lot of people, a lot of Jewish girls and a lot of Jewish boys, they came out. And we were afraid to stay there while they are pulling already the Germans from Poland back to Germany. And the soldiers, the Americans, they stayed. Because they're going to kill us-- the Gentile, they're going to kill us if we're going to beat them. So whatever they went, we went with them. With the American army?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the American army. And they the one that freed us. So we went-- in the end, we went to-- they kept us-- they liberated-- we were in Dachau. They liberated Dachau already. And there were a lot of the Jewish people, they were starving and everything. They were looking like skeletons, completely like skin and bone. And so a lot of them, they had to go-- on starvation, like we were on starvation. You know, I was like skin and bone. So when I went from the houses to the bunkers, but I was happy. I found a family. I have already a family. They were to me like a sister and brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then we were again on starvation, so when they start to give us some food, the American soldiers, the American food, all different cheeses and butter and whatever, the cans and everything. And they told us to eat very slowly, to learn like a baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because some of them being in the Dachau in the concentration camp, they are liberated, and when they saw a piece of bread, when they saw a piece of cheese or whatever, so they grabbed it very fast, and they couldn't take it, and they died. A lot of them died after, you know, because they were starting to eat very fast. And then they took us to Austria. And the-- the American soldiers, we were there before they were there in the barracks. So they-- they moved out from the barracks, and they put us, the Jewish people, to the barracks, and we were there in Linz in Austria. And then it was like UNRRA from United States. It became organization UNRRA. And they used to send to the care packages to this. It became organization. And they used to give us every week in those barracks, the Americans. We stood in the line, and they used to give us a [NON-ENGLISH], like little packages from week to week. We had to live on it under American zone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So let me ask you this. When were you liberated in the Ukraine by the Americans? Do you remember the month and year?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in-- it was beginning like in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so in the winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because they pulled already out from Russia, the Germans, pulling back to Germany, the leftovers. So while they were there with the American soldiers, we went with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you followed the American soldiers West?

speaker: interviewee
text: We followed the American soldiers, yeah. We didn't want to stay in the back in Europe because we were afraid. But that time, it became already more and more, I was not the only one, Jewish girl at that time. So we went to, you know, to the-- on the Austria zone. And then over there, I met my husband. It was like a bazaar, was like a market. Like, we were laughing. They say, I bought my husband on the supermarket. So I met him. I went and I used to-- I was bare feet. I was still very, you know, hungry and in starvation, still yet in bare feet. And nobody was-- all in the middle by myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went on the bazaar with a friend of mine, on the market. And he was with a friend. And we started talking. And he felt sorry for me, so he bought right away a pair of slippers. You know? And he gave me an American [NON-ENGLISH] they called. The soldiers that they're wearing their long coats and green color, so he bought a coat. He gave it to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he says to me, you are alone and I'm alone. We both survived and everything. Let's get together. And that time, I didn't know what it means to look for education or personality or this, and I said, OK. So we were in a house in the bunk-- in the barracks, and there were like 10 people. And the men said that he was a rabbi or something. And he would wrote on a piece of paper, you know, like [NON-ENGLISH]. So [INAUDIBLE] married me. I didn't marry myself. He married me. I didn't know what means to be married, what means love or what anything. I didn't know. I was so naive. I was so childish that I don't know why I was rushing, why I was grabbing, because I was still bare feet. I had nothing on me. I was so poor. And the food was not enough. Like, they gave a [NON-ENGLISH] just for a week. So we had to every little day, every day, just today, so much a little, and so much a little. That should last for a whole week. And it's sad that started.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the people that were in the bunker with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came all together. They were with us together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with them together in Austria in the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the bunkers, yeah. They were already from Dachau that they are liberated, the people. So they took them-- a lot of them died from starvation because they start right away to eat. And they were so skin and bone. Just collect from people. So they came there too, and they-- it was like three, four blocks. There were a lot. So there were quite a few, the Jewish people that--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you to get from your bunker in the Ukraine back into Germany, into Dachau, following the American Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it it was-- it was-- we went by trains. It was a long way from Ukraine to Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the war was still going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: The war was-- they're pulling the Germans back to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you followed them?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we followed. They had liberated Poland already, and we followed them, yes. We were afraid to stay in the back and in the same city because there were hardly any Jew or Jews [INAUDIBLE]. And we were afraid they're going, the Ukrainian and the Polish, if we're going to stay, remain there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you got to Dachau after the liberation, then, of Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: The American soldiers are liberated them. So we were there while they were liberated them because it was Germany, and they came already to Germany because the American soldiers went all the way to Russia to fight with Russia against the Germans. So they pulled back from Russia, from Poland, all the way to Germany. And I was there when they liberated the other people too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in '45. I think in May in '45. They liberated us. So we were already became like one group. And then all the people from Dachau, whatever was left over, and us, and everything, they took us to-- and the Austrians-- to Linz. I don't know why not in Germany. They were afraid. Of what, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your reaction upon seeing the concentration camp, upon seeing Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, they took us-- first of all, they took us to Majdanek. Then Majdanek was in Poland, in Lublin. It was a concentration camp too. And that was when they are liberated. And I saw this, everything in Majdanek. The Jew, the skeletons, full of still-- they were still. Not alive. Dead. And then I saw what they made from the Jewish skin everything. They made soap, I did see. And then they caught three Germans that they were SS, and they brought us, the Jewish people that we are liberated. The American soldiers brought us there to see how they're hanging the German officers. They brought us on a open truck. And then they put it with the string on top, and the way they're hanging them. And they want us to see it, and we should say something about them. So a lot of-- we were happy. We were like, laughing and very happy. And some Jewish people, they were spitting on them. They were-- because their hands were tied up. Their feet already were tied up. And it was almost before they pulled away already the truck, they were by the rope hanging. And they were spitting on them. They were kicking them with their feet and everything. But we were unhappy that it's only three. It's not-- should have been- so we saw Majdanek. And then after that, we went to-- they took us to freedom. And we were there for-- we were there for four years in Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's pause here. This is the end of tape number four in an interview with Edith Cimmer.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm almost in the--
